TITLE: A survey on accessibility awareness of Brazilian web developers
RQ1:
* The accessibility evaluation mechanisms most commonly used by developers are not truly accessibility evaluation mechanisms, but rather compliance with Web standards.

* Lack of training

* Addresses the general lack of awareness among developers regarding digital accessibility in several sections

* Most developers reported that they have never heard of Web Accessibility Guidelines;

TITLE: A survey on the accessibility awareness of people involved in web development projects in Brazil
RQ1:
* Lack of awareness about laws/decrees: approximately 73% of developers are not fully aware of Law No. 5,296/2004

* Lack of awareness about guidelines: 39.15% are not familiar with WCAG, and 30.18% have only basic knowledge of it

* Lack of training: 56.38% of participants have never received any training on accessibility

* Suggestions to improve web accessibility: disseminate knowledge about accessibility legislation and increase awareness on this topic by providing accessibility training within organizations
RQ2:
* Regarding the consideration of accessibility in the projects they participate in: only 19.9% fully consider accessibility in their projects; 44.37% consider it partially, and 35.4% do not consider it at all

* When participants consider accessibility in their projects, personal motivation is the main contributing factor. In contrast, company or client motivation has the lowest contribution

* Main reasons for not considering accessibility in their projects: lack of formal requirements from the organization (51.33%) and lack of requirements from clients (49.08%)
RQ3: * Use of accessibility evaluation tools: 47.7% stated that they do not use any accessibility evaluation tools in their projects. Among those who do use some tool, they are not exactly accessibility evaluation methods (HTML and CSS validation)

TITLE: A survey on the awareness of brazilian web development community about cognitive accessibility
Challenges: * For those who do not consider people with cognitive disabilities in their projects, the main challenges and barriers: 75.3% cited a lack of knowledge about cognitive disabilities
RQ1:
* Regarding knowledge of accessibility guidelines: 33% of respondents stated that they are not familiar with WCAG; 13% indicated that they have never used it (i.e., they know it but have never used it)

* Regarding other complementary materials that support the implementation of accessibility requirements: scientific studies and technical blogs are unknown to only 8.6%

* For those who consider people with cognitive disabilities in their projects, which main cognitive disabilities they consider: 62.5% consider people with intellectual disabilities; ASD and ADHD both reached 56.3% among respondents
RQ2:
* Regarding the consideration of people with cognitive disabilities in their projects: 54.3% of respondents stated that they do not consider these individuals in their projects, and 30.5% indicated that they consider them partially

* For those who consider individuals with cognitive disabilities, their motivations are: 62.5% indicated that they understand these individuals are part of their audience; 62.5% stated that they consider this group to allow them to benefit from their product or service; 56.3% reported personal motivations

* For those who do not consider people with cognitive disabilities in their projects, the main challenges and barriers are: 53.9% stated that this group is not considered in their projects by the organization

* Respondents have difficulty understanding that people with cognitive disabilities are also part of their target audience

* For those who do not consider accessibility in their projects, what could motivate them to consider this group in future projects: 79.8% stated that guidelines could motivate them; 67.4% said that increasing knowledge about cognitive disabilities could also help in this process
RQ3: * For those who consider people with cognitive disabilities in their projects, which materials they use to ensure accessibility for this group: 75% use WCAG as support, while 50% use technical blogs and scientific studies

TITLE: Perspectives and Practices of Digital Accessibility: A Survey of User Experience Professionals in Nordic Countries
Challenges: * Main challenges: time constraints, lack of training, and cost. These are associated with a lack of management support, as management is responsible for budget, workload allocation, and training
RQ1:
* It is worth noting that participants had greater experience in accessibility and are leading UX professionals in their countries

* Regarding their expertise in digital accessibility, most consider themselves at an intermediate level, while a small portion consider themselves experts

* Most participants were aware of accessibility legislation and follow it

* It was observed that knowledge of digital accessibility is higher among UX professionals and designers, and lower among managers and developers

* Education and training are essential for the adoption of accessibility. Lack of training was one of the main barriers to creating accessible systems
RQ2:
* The main reason organizations include accessibility in their projects is legal obligation

* Participants stated that professionals with expertise in interface design, usability design, and front-end development should be responsible for digital accessibility practices. Management roles were also considered responsible for making systems accessible, while back-end developers were the least cited group for this responsibility

* In addition to legal obligation, other common motivations for including accessibility were ethical principles and personal motivations
RQ3: * The most commonly used accessibility evaluation tools were HTML validation and CSS validation; however, they do not require users to test the system’s accessibility features

TITLE: Strategic Insights into Integrating Accessibility in Software Development: Motivations, Barriers, Commitments, and Business Value
RQ1: * Interviewees highlight that although policies are aligned and designers and developers are committed to implementing accessibility requirements, development is complex and inherently prone to errors
RQ2:
* The study highlights that demands initiated and supported by tactical and strategic layers of organizations are essential to enable the development of accessible software

* It is further stated that, while the commitment of all professionals involved in the organizational process is fundamental, support from strategic layers leads to plans, policies, guidelines, and investments that ensure organizations achieve their goals
RQ3:
* One of the main measures adopted is the inclusion of accessibility criteria in the “Definition of Done” (DoD), ensuring that all stakeholders understand, implement, and validate accessibility requirements throughout development, rather than only at the end

* Many participants mentioned that organizations enforce policies requiring new requirements and artifacts to be validated by the accessibility team. Additionally, organizations also impose mandatory accessibility testing to ensure these requirements are properly met

TITLE: Studying industry practices of accessibility requirements in agile development
Challenges:
* The main challenge mentioned when trying to specify accessibility requirements was the lack of experts in the team, lack of team knowledge, and lack of tools

* For those who rely on guidelines in the process, some find the language too complex for non-experts
RQ1:
* Level of accessibility knowledge: 38.5% basic knowledge; 30.8% intermediate; 15% no experience

* Regarding training provided by companies on accessibility: 53.8% rarely received training; 38.5% have never received any training

* 53.8% have never used standards or guidelines as a resource to support the specification, development, and testing of accessibility

* Some companies consider accessibility to comply with local legislation. In the context of the study, 53.8% of companies are not familiar with these obligations; 30.8% are slightly familiar, and 15.4% are moderately familiar

* Most participants are not very familiar with assistive technologies (61.6%)

* Lack of training (30.8%) was also mentioned as a factor contributing to the lack of prioritization of accessibility requirements
RQ2:
* Legal obligation (30.8%) was cited as the main motivation for companies to implement accessibility requirements

* Regarding the prioritization of accessibility requirements: 69.2% stated that fewer than five projects they participated in prioritized accessibility requirements; 30.8% have never treated accessibility as a priority in their projects

* In general, participants reported that the lack of enforcement by clients (61.5%) is the main factor for not considering these requirements (a common aspect in agile development)
RQ3:
* Most commonly used techniques for specifying accessibility requirements: acceptance criteria (5/13) and user stories (3/13)

* Regarding accessibility evaluation methods: 81.4% of responses indicated little or no familiarity with these methods

* Among those who reported being familiar, the most used approaches are automated tools (38.5%) and manual reviews of accessibility guidelines (38.5%), followed by testing with people with disabilities (30.8%), expert evaluation (23.1%), and HTML and CSS validation (23.1%)

* Models, checklists, and catalogs could support agile teams in specifying accessibility requirements

TITLE: Why Software is Not Accessible: Technology Professionals' Perspectives and Challenges
Challenges: * They mention the difficulty of retroactively evaluating accessibility issues during development cycles, especially in large-scale projects
RQ1:
* It states that, due to the lack of formal education, professionals had limited knowledge about accessibility, compliance requirements, or guidelines. Only 35% of respondents learned something substantial about accessibility during their formal education

* Regarding familiarity with accessibility guidelines and U.S. accessibility laws (ADA): 44% are slightly familiar and 63% are not familiar at all

* They mention difficulties in interpreting guidelines and express a desire for guidelines to provide demonstrations on how to implement accessible components, rather than only describing them
RQ2:
* Leadership, especially in smaller companies, sometimes focuses on economic goals, so accessibility is considered an additional cost in terms of time and resources

* Developers tend to incorporate company values, which do not always include accessibility as a priority. However, if leadership positions valued accessibility as part of their objectives, participants would be more likely to have access to resources to include accessibility

* Some respondents stated that accessibility was neglected amid frequent task switching, within a culture of tight deadlines driven by the constant pursuit of industry trends

* Regarding the tools or resources professionals use to create accessible solutions: in the survey, accessibility is, in most cases, not considered throughout the development lifecycle
RQ3:
* Regarding the tools or resources professionals use to create accessible solutions: in the interviews, they indicated that the high pressure of development cycles demands simple and easy-to-use tools

* Regarding tools: participants stated that there is a lack of tools and resources, especially examples of how to create accessible components

* Some participants mentioned preferring better tools that can identify accessibility violations and provide quick solutions

TITLE: Accessibility challenges and tool features: an IBM Web developer perspective
Challenges:
* Major difficulties reported when developing accessible solutions: design, testing, and finding technical alternatives to overcome limitations.

* Although most interviewed developers have access to information and training, testing and finding alternatives to overcome limitations require significant time, and lack of time is a limiting factor in making systems accessible;

* Lack of time is the most common and limiting reason for creating an accessible solution.

* Applying accessibility retroactively is difficult and time-consuming.

TITLE: Mobile Accessibility Guidelines Adoption under the Perspective of Developers and Designers
RQ2:
* Regarding the reasons why guidelines are not applied in projects: client priorities, project time constraints, and the priorities of the company developing the system;

* Obstacles: lack of prioritization by stakeholders and limited awareness among decision-makers, such as clients.

TITLE: How Web Professionals Perceive Web Accessibility in Practice: Active Roles, Process Phases and Key Disabilities
Web accessibility in Turkey: awareness, understanding and practices of user experience professionals
RQ3: * Accessibility evaluation methods used by participants included testing with people with disabilities (15 participants) and checking compliance with accessibility guidelines (8 participants). Other reported methods were the use of assistive technologies (n = 4), CSS validation (n = 4), HTML validation (n = 4), and testing with older adults (n = 4). No participants reported using automated accessibility evaluation tools;

TITLE: Understanding developer challenges and trends in web accessibility: a stack overflow analysis
Challenges:
*Statistical Focus and Impact: The analysis identified that discussions revolve around three main aspects: flexibility and efficiency of time-based content, keyboard navigation, and aesthetic design, which together account for more than 60% of the reported accessibility issues;

* Despite the availability of a wide range of guidelines, developers may face obstacles in making websites accessible, particularly in adopting accessibility best practices, such as assigning accessibility attributes for screen readers and other tools, or handling accessibility-related warnings.
RQ1:
* Visual accessibility as a foundation: Considering various aspects to accommodate people with visual impairments (including blindness) can serve as an initial step for developers to understand and implement all types of accessibility requirements;

* Web developers lack knowledge of how assistive technologies work. This highlights the absence of comprehensive guidelines to understand how individuals with disabilities interact with web applications.
RQ2: * Although the analyzed posts mainly focus on non-visual access, there is evidence that developers are also concerned with other topics related to supporting different types of disabilities.
RQ3:
* Introducing accessibility-related discussions on Stack Overflow could facilitate the dissemination and enrichment of knowledge on this critical topic;

* This suggests that the availability of standalone guidelines may be insufficient, as a more practical approach—along with user-friendly tools and platforms—could be essential to guide developers through the complex process of achieving web accessibility.
